Building a Small Project as a HexSchool Intern
Looking back on the process and experience of building the HexSchool Talent Wall during my time as a HexSchool intern. Lots of nervousness and fear all the way through, aaaa~

The Talent Wall went live safely the other day. Honestly I was nervous about launch — past experience taught me that systems love breaking right before launch, or even minutes before. Thankfully it went live smoothly.
I joined HexSchool as a frontend intern on 5/28. Pretty much my first task from teacher Liao was to build the HexSchool Talent Wall (uh, wait?!).
When I first got the assignment, I had no idea where to start. So teacher Liao first walked me through the flow. A lot of it overturned my old dev mental model.
For example: previously when I built systems, I'd hear the requirements, plan the features, then think about which fields each feature needed, then how to implement them.
But for the Talent Wall, the planning used a different mindset: User Stories.
The User Story concept is genuinely simple but kind of magical — a single sentence can fan out into a complete feature set. For the Talent Wall:
As a job-seeker, I want a platform that showcases me.
The base requirement: job-seeker data is stored on GitHub as JSON.
Then we extended from this User Story.
Job-seekers need a showcase platform. Base requirement: all job-seekers must post their data on GitHub. They also need to provide job-search info — otherwise how do you showcase your job search?

So we discussed what fields a job-seeker would need: name, preferred location, tech tags, etc. We discussed the fields together — solo discussion misses details. The initial version of the fields (I forgot to include short descriptions originally — added later) ↓
language-json shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

imgUrl:     Job-seeker photo link (image size: 150x150)
name:       Job-seeker name
tags:       Job-seeker skills (max 4)
job:        Position being applied for (max 2)
location:   Preferred location — by region name (max 4)
type:       Job type (only these 3 types allowed)
experience: Work experience (numeric only)
profileUrl: Online resume link — must include demo + source code (GitHub)
location:   Preferred location — by region name (max
profileUrl: Online resume link — must include demo + source code (GitHub)

After fields, the initial UI plan. Teacher Liao suggested using Bootstrap Card components. I have to admit I have no UI design instinct. With the teacher's explanations and direction, I slowly figured out how to lay out the fields to look decent (didn't screenshot anything, so no images, QQ).
After fields + UI, the posting rules. Otherwise people might just randomly post — since all postings go through teacher review, we needed a posting guideline doc.
While I was writing this, the teacher recommended a book called "Lightning Development: Standing on the windfall to win the market, the golden formula for going from 0 to NT$10B".
A lot of ideas in it overturned my thinking. My original Talent Wall posting rules looked like this ↓
Talent Wall posting rules — initial version
I spent about a week on the book — hard to digest at once. Teacher Liao pointed me at a specific chapter: "Conversational Design". The idea:
. Simple. When the teacher first looked at my guideline, he immediately asked: "Is this HexSchool recruiting?" That made me realize my phrasing was off. So I changed it to "Posting Info".
When building the UI, it wasn't done in one shot. It iterated, inspired by the same "Lightning Development" mindset.
The build wasn't that hard, so I had a first version quickly ↓
The teacher reviewed it and pointed out the headline first: "Find your outstanding talent" was the wrong framing.
We wanted businesses to quickly find the nearest matching talent. So the headline should be "Find the talent nearest to you" — way better fit. So the headline became "
Find the talent nearest to you
V1 was just a baseline. V2 had real adjustments — not just UI, but also posting fields.

Originally I thought a "short description" field wasn't important and dropped it. But after the teacher explained, I realized it was actually important. So a short description was added to the posting info. The teacher also pointed out issues with my doc.
He asked: "When a user comes in, the first thing they see is 'HexSchool Talent Wall'. OK, and then? What is this for?"
That hit me. After discussion, I added a line below explaining the Wall's purpose:
This is the HexSchool Talent Wall provided by HexSchool. Every person listed here is verified by HexSchool. By posting here, your job-search info is exposed to all hiring partners directly.
Also added that signup takes just a few simple steps — nothing complicated.
UI-wise, the colors were genuinely NO GOOD, so I tweaked them to this ↓
Through this iteration the product matured. Next adjustment was color. Teacher Wang Zhicheng also gave input:
don't pick colors at random — without a clear focus, your highlights don't pop
. Final UI ended up like this ↓
(There were further color tweaks when integrating into the main site. Honestly, color is just not my thing, Orz.)
Then the standard system test phase. We grabbed a few candidates for a closed beta (lol). Since posting requires basic Git, plenty of friction was expected.
This test phase was super important. Things I thought weren't important turned out to matter a lot. From testing we found most people had these issues:
Where do I put my profile pic? Where do I host it?
How do I count short description characters?
I don't know Git commands — what do I do?
How do I send a PR?

So I added correct formats, image hosting recommendations, character counting, and clearer rules to the doc. To help posting go smoothly, I also wrote three articles on basic Git, how to send a PR, and how to resolve conflicts — and attached them to the doc.
(The conflicts article has its own backstory — coming later.)
The testing phase was about making sure the rules doc clearly tells users what to do. As the developer running through the flow yourself, you can't catch all the issues.
The Talent Wall had to integrate into the HexSchool main site, so I had to find who currently owned the main-site integration. I asked Leaf, who sits next to me.

The Talent Wall was written in vanilla JavaScript, but the HexSchool site uses CoffeeScript. So I spent about four hours converting my vanilla JS to CoffeeScript to avoid bothering Leaf with conversion work — and to cut down on his modifications.
What the final integration looks like — go check the main site ~
Bonus — the backstory of the "resolving conflicts" article

Since I didn't know how to manufacture or resolve Git conflicts at first, I asked Leaf to deliberately create some conflicts for me. That got me posted into the HexSchool "ridiculous moments" log by the boss, ˊ_>ˋ
In the end Leaf told me: "I've cracked the knack for deliberately creating conflicts!" (uh?????)
